steps.title: Identify the structure on each side
steps.content: Left: "Not all plants benefit equally or from the same kind of ______" — an independent clause making a general claim. Right: "alfalfa and forsythia, for example, benefit from endomycorrhizal associations; many tree species...benefit from ectomycorrhizal associations; and plants in the Ericaceae family...don't benefit from mycorrhizae at all" — three independent clauses that illustrate the claim.

steps.title: Apply the rule: a colon introduces illustrative examples after an independent clause
steps.content:
• Choice A — mycorrhizae;: "...same kind of mycorrhizae; alfalfa and forsythia..." — a semicolon joins two independent clauses as equals, but the right side elaborates on the left, not adds a parallel thought. ✗
  • Choice B — mycorrhizae: "...same kind of mycorrhizae alfalfa and forsythia..." — no punctuation runs both sides together as a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — mycorrhizae,: "...same kind of mycorrhizae, alfalfa and forsythia..." — a comma between two independent clauses is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — mycorrhizae:: "...same kind of mycorrhizae: alfalfa and forsythia, for example, benefit from endomycorrhizal associations..." — the colon correctly follows the IC and introduces the three illustrating examples. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
